summary: DHH highlights and endorses blog posts by Chris Petrilli and James Robertson that critique enterprise software culture. The post amplifies their arguments that big IT budgets reflect architectural bloat and turf wars rather than success, and that most applications are far simpler than the enterprise industry wants people to believe. DHH frames complexity addiction as a disease spread by enterprise jargon and consulting culture.
key_points: Large IT budgets typically signal runaway architecture and organizational dysfunction, not project success
Most IT budgets could be cut 30-70% by simply saying 'no' to unnecessary complexity
Most applications are not nearly as complicated as analysts, vendors, and 'propeller heads' claim
Enterprise complexity is self-perpetuating, driven by those who profit from it
money_quotes: The addiction to complexity is a disease and the enterprisey babble is its vector.
Huge budgets are usually indicative of run-away architecture and absurd turf-wars and protectionism, not actual success.
Most IT budgets I've been around could be shaved 30-70% by the judicious use of the word 'no,' and the refusal to accept stock answers from astronauts and certification-wielding automatons.
Most applications just aren't that complicated. The propeller heads would like you to think they are - too many analysts want you to think they are - and too many vendors want you to think they are.
Chris, James, and others are doing a great job helping to fight the contamination.
key_insight: Enterprise software complexity is largely artificial, sustained by vendors, consultants, and architects who profit from making simple problems appear difficult.
